Here is a thing that, I suspect, keeps a lot of hedge fund lawyers up at ni=
ght. Your hedge fund is in the business of getting =E2=80=9Cedge.=E2=80=9D =
It wants to know things that other people do not know, in order to buy the =
stocks that will go up. One way to get edge is to bribe the assistant treas=
urer of a public company to give you the company=E2=80=99s earnings release=
=C2=A0in advance, but this is strongly disfavored and your hedge fund has e=
ffective=C2=A0policies against it. Another way to get edge, quite popular t=
hese days, is to buy =E2=80=9Calternative data.=E2=80=9D Somebody has a sat=
ellite and they fly it over mall parking lots and count up the cars in fron=
t of each store, and you pay them and they give you=C2=A0those numbers, and=
 then you buy the stocks of the stores with lots of cars in front of them, =
and then a month later those stores announce good earnings and you make a p=
rofit.The car-counting is a classic story of alternative data, but the way =
alternative data mostly works in practice is that a lot of people use apps =
on their mobile phones, and everyone involved in the mobile app business is=
 harvesting data and frantically selling it to each other and to hedge fund=
s. So basically the =E2=80=9Calternative data=E2=80=9D that your hedge fund=
 buys is of the form =E2=80=9Cthis many people used this company=E2=80=99s =
app,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cpeople spent this much on this company=E2=80=99s =
products through apps,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0or =E2=80=9Cphone location data shows =
that this many people walked into this store,=E2=80=9D or whatever. Someone=
 in the app business collects that data and then sells it to hedge funds, i=
ncluding yours. And you use this mobile-app data to get a sense of how many=
 people are interacting with some company, so you can buy or sell that comp=
any=E2=80=99s stock.Now, if you bribe the company=E2=80=99s assistant treas=
urer to get an early peek at an=C2=A0earnings release, that is clearly =E2=
=80=9Cinsider trading.=E2=80=9D It is illegal, the bad kind of edge. As I  =
like to say, insider trading is not about fairness; it=E2=80=99s about thef=
t.=C2=A0Someone (the company) owns the information (the earnings release); =
somebody else (the assistant treasurer) takes it, in breach of a duty to th=
e owners of the information,=C2=A0and gives it to you illegitimately. So yo=
u are trading on illegally obtained information and get in trouble.What abo=
ut that mobile-app data that you=E2=80=99re buying from an alternative data=
 provider? Well, it is=C2=A0nonpublic=C2=A0information: Not everyone has it=
, which is why you want it; it gives you an edge.[1] But the question is wh=
ether you got it illegitimately, which means, whether the data broker owned=
 the data and had the right to sell it to you. I think that is a complicate=
d question? Roughly speaking:=09If the people using the apps give the apps =
permission to track them, and the companies that make the apps give permiss=
ion to the aggregators to aggregate them, and the aggregators give permissi=
on to the data vendors=C2=A0to sell their data, and the vendors sell the da=
ta to you, fine, great. Everybody has consented to the use of their informa=
tion, so you can use it freely.=09If at any point in that chain =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0which can have more or fewer links than I wrote=C2=A0in the previous =
paragraph =E2=80=94=C2=A0someone=C2=A0doesn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0give permission,=
 then the data is misappropriated. If you use that data in your trading, yo=
u are insider trading,=C2=A0trading based on misappropriated material nonpu=
blic information. Also the person doing the misappropriating =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0the person passing along data without permission =E2=80=94=C2=A0is also =
engaged in insider trading, if they know that you=E2=80=99re going to be us=
ing it to trade.So if an app says =E2=80=9Cwe don=E2=80=99t sell your data,=
=E2=80=9D and then it sells that data to hedge funds, that data is arguably=
 misappropriated? Arguably it is material nonpublic information that belong=
s=C2=A0to the users of the app, and is sold to hedge funds without their pe=
rmission? Or if people click a box in an app saying =E2=80=9Csure go nuts s=
ell my data to anyone,=E2=80=9D and the app sells the data to some third-pa=
rty service, but the app=E2=80=99s contract with the third-party service sa=
ys =E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t sell any of this data, in an identifiable form, t=
o hedge funds,=E2=80=9D and the service does that, then, again, that=E2=80=
=99s misappropriation and possible insider trading.And so as the general co=
unsel of a hedge fund you do due diligence, and you ask the alternative dat=
a provider questions like =E2=80=9Cdid everyone give informed consent to yo=
u collecting this data and selling it to me, a hedge fund?=E2=80=9D And the=
y say yes, and you hope they=E2=80=99re telling the truth, and you examine =
their contracts and the apps=E2=80=99 terms of service and so forth to make=
 sure that the chain of data is reasonably clean. But you probably always w=
orry a bit because, I don=E2=80=99t know, the whole business of apps sellin=
g data to each other does seem like it is not founded on best practices of =
fully informed consent?Yesterday the=C2=A0U.S. Securities and Exchange Comm=
ission brought a pretty important enforcement action against a company call=
ed App Annie Inc. and its former chief executive officer, Bertrand Schmitt;=
 Schmitt and App Annie settled for about $10 million. App Annie got data fr=
om apps and sold it to hedge funds without using best practices of fully in=
formed consent:According to the SEC's order, App Annie is one of the larges=
t sellers of market data on mobile app performance, including estimates on =
the number of times a particular company's app is downloaded, how often it'=
s used, and the amount of revenue the app generates for the company. Tradin=
g firms commonly refer to this type of information as "alternative data" be=
cause it is not contained within companies' financial statements or other t=
raditional data sources. The order finds that App Annie and Schmitt underst=
ood that companies would only share their confidential app performance data=
 with App Annie if it promised not to disclose their data to third parties,=
 and as a result App Annie and Schmitt assured companies that their data wo=
uld be aggregated and anonymized before being used by a statistical model t=
o generate estimates of app performance. Contrary to these representations,=
 the order finds that from late 2014 through mid-2018, App Annie used non-a=
ggregated and non-anonymized data to alter its model-generated estimates to=
 make them more valuable to sell to trading firms.The order further finds t=
hat App Annie and Schmitt misrepresented to their trading firm customers th=
at App Annie generated the estimates in a way that was consistent with the =
consents it obtained from companies that shared their confidential data, an=
d that App Annie had effective internal controls to prevent the misuse of c=
onfidential data and to ensure that it was in compliance with the federal s=
ecurities laws. According to the SEC's order, App Annie and Schmitt were aw=
are that trading firm customers were making investment decisions based on A=
pp Annie's estimates, and App Annie also shared ideas for how the trading f=
irms could use the estimates to trade ahead of upcoming earnings announceme=
nts.It is a little hard to tell what people=C2=A0thought=C2=A0was going on.=
 Basically if you are a company with an app, you can use an App Annie produ=
ct for free to, like, see how good your app is:App Annie provides a free an=
alytics product called =E2=80=9CConnect=E2=80=9D to companies that offer ap=
ps, including public companies, which enables those companies to track how =
their apps are performing. As a condition of their use of Connect, those co=
mpanies provide App Annie with their app store credentials to allow App Ann=
ie to collect their confidential app performance metrics (=E2=80=9CConnect =
Data=E2=80=9D). App Annie tells Connect users that it will generate estimat=
es of app performance using their Connect Data, but that it will only use C=
onnect Data in aggregated and anonymized form to generate those estimates.A=
nd if you are a hedge fund you can buy App Annie=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cestimat=
es=E2=80=9D of app performance, which you can then use as part of your own =
modeling of the company=E2=80=99s financial performance:App Annie=E2=80=99s=
 business model relied on selling estimates of how apps belonging to certai=
n companies were performing. App Annie sold these estimates through a paid =
subscription product called =E2=80=9CIntelligence,=E2=80=9D which included =
=E2=80=9CStore Intelligence=E2=80=9D (for estimates of app revenue and app =
downloads) and =E2=80=9CUsage Intelligence=E2=80=9D (for estimates of app u=
sage). Paying subscribers were companies that offered apps and trading firm=
s, such as hedge funds. During the relevant period, substantially all of Ap=
p Annie=E2=80=99s revenue was derived from selling Intelligence estimates t=
o these subscribers. =E2=80=A6App Annie=E2=80=99s marketing materials encou=
raged =E2=80=9Cinvestors=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cfinance professionals=E2=80=
=9D to use Intelligence estimates to =E2=80=9Cinform their investment strat=
egy.=E2=80=9D App Annie=E2=80=99s website claimed that investors could =E2=
=80=9Cmake more informed decisions about existing positions=E2=80=9D by =E2=
=80=9Cbenchmark[ing] the performance of public app companies against key me=
trics including user engagement, revenue and growth.=E2=80=9D Schmitt occas=
ionally participated in meetings with existing and prospective trading firm=
 subscribers, and some of the materials used in these meetings pitched that=
 Intelligence estimates could help with =E2=80=9Cfinancial modeling,=E2=80=
=9D =E2=80=9Csharpen earnings forecasts with estimated downloads and revenu=
e inputs,=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cidentify investment opportunities.=E2=80=9D=
As far as I can tell these =E2=80=9Cestimates=E2=80=9D worked like this:=09=
App Annie used aggregated anonymous data from the apps it tracks to build a=
 statistical model of how apps with certain characteristics perform.=09It u=
sed that model to generate an estimate of how each particular company=E2=80=
=99s app performed.=09Then it checked that estimate against=C2=A0how the ap=
p actually performed, which it knew, from collecting=C2=A0that app=E2=80=99=
s data, and corrected the estimate to reflect the actual performance.Arguab=
ly steps (1) and (2) were unnecessary, from a performance-estimation standp=
oint? Like, you just have the answer; why build a statistical model to esti=
mate it?=C2=A0But they were important from a selling-data-to-hedge-funds st=
andpoint: App Annie =E2=80=9Crepresented to Intelligence subscribers that I=
ntelligence estimates were generated through a statistical model that used =
aggregated and anonymized Connect Data, and that Connect users had consente=
d to App Annie using their confidential data in this way.=E2=80=9D And in f=
act the Connect users =E2=80=94=C2=A0the companies =E2=80=94=C2=A0had=C2=A0=
consented to App Annie using their data in that way.[2] But that=E2=80=99s =
not how App Annie used it, or at least, not the only way:Beginning at least=
 in 2014, in an effort to make the Intelligence estimates closer to the act=
ual app performance metrics, App Annie created a manual process whereby a =
=E2=80=9CDelivery Team,=E2=80=9D which consisted of a subset of App Annie e=
ngineers based in Beijing, China, made manual alterations to estimates gene=
rated by the statistical model before they were delivered to Intelligence s=
ubscribers. Schmitt was aware of and approved the creation and use of this =
manual alteration process.The Delivery Team manually altered the estimates =
for apps that were of greatest interest to App Annie=E2=80=99s highest-payi=
ng subscribers. When App Annie received complaints from subscribers about t=
he inaccuracy of a particular company=E2=80=99s app performance estimates, =
the Delivery Team was tasked with improving the estimates through this manu=
al process.=C2=A0To make these manual alterations, the Delivery Team looked=
 at confidential Connect Data, including public company app performance dat=
a. The Delivery Team was not trained or supervised by anyone in the Company=
=E2=80=99s Data Science group and did not document which estimates were adj=
usted or what changes were made. There was no statistical basis for these p=
ost-model alterations. The only purpose of these alterations was to make th=
e estimates closer to the actual metrics.Schmitt approved the creation and =
use of this manual alteration process because he believed it was cheaper an=
d more effective at making the estimates closer to the actual results than =
a process that would have complied with the Connect Terms of Service, such =
as having data scientists research and implement improvements to the statis=
tical model itself.=C2=A0I =E2=80=A6=C2=A0honestly I sort of sympathize? If=
 you know how much revenue a company got through its app, it seems a bit ba=
roque to have a team of data scientists build a statistical model based on =
data from hundreds of apps, and refine that model in order to make it more =
accurately estimate how much revenue the company got through its app, when =
you can just look at the answer. Like, yes, telling people the actual data=
=C2=A0is=C2=A0cheaper and more effective at making the estimates closer to =
the actual results than some more complicated process that does not involve=
=C2=A0looking at the actual results.=C2=A0This is a very simple business, r=
eally, that was rendered a lot more complicated by the desire to wave vague=
ly in the direction of securities-law compliance.Anyway it=E2=80=99s all fi=
ne now:In June 2018, after the Company had learned of the SEC=E2=80=99s inv=
estigation, App Annie discontinued all post-model estimate alteration pract=
ices based on non-aggregated and non-anonymized Connect Data. The Company a=
lso began excluding all public company data from its statistical model, con=
sistent with its representations to Intelligence subscribers and Connect us=
ers. Around the same time, Schmitt resigned and the Board replaced him as C=
EO.App Annie eventually implemented a new version of the statistical model =
underlying the Store Intelligence product that had been proposed by the Chi=
ef Data Scientist in or around mid-2016.I actually feel like if you are a h=
edge fund lawyer this case probably comes as a relief to you? Like, yes, yo=
u were buying illegal alternative data, but (1) the data was good and (2)=
=C2=A0you=C2=A0didn=E2=80=99t get in trouble. App Annie got in trouble, for=
 lying to you, rather than you getting in trouble, for believing them.Which=
 seems fair and good, don=E2=80=99t get me wrong, but you were the one doin=
g the trading. It is not necessarily=C2=A0intuitive=C2=A0that an app-perfor=
mance-analytics-and-data-aggregation company would get in trouble for=C2=A0=
securities fraud=C2=A0for using non-aggregated data in its estimates of app=
 performance. App Annie wasn=E2=80=99t buying or selling securities. SEC Co=
mmissioner Hester Peirce tweeted in dissent: =E2=80=9CThis settlement stret=
ches the =E2=80=98in connection with the purchase and sale of securities=E2=
=80=99 requirement under 10b/10b-5 beyond where I think it should go.=E2=80=
=9D[3] I disagree: I think that this is a=C2=A0subtle insider trading case,=
 and the SEC was clever=C2=A0to pursue it and to correctly=C2=A0identify=C2=
=A0the bad guy (App Annie, which lied to hedge funds to sell illegal data, =
not the hedge funds, which were deceived and bought the data). But it is no=
t obvious. And I suppose that if you are a hedge fund lawyer, now you are o=
n notice that you should be asking your alternative data providers some tou=
gher questions.

      GreenSky
   =20
 =20
I just want to predict right now that at some point in the future someone i=
s going to borrow money from Goldman Sachs Group Inc. to pay an unexpected =
medical expense, and they=E2=80=99re going to have a hard time paying Goldm=
an Sachs back, and Goldman Sachs is going to be=C2=A0very=C2=A0understandin=
g and helpful in working with them but it will try to get its money back, a=
nd despite all of Goldman Sachs=E2=80=99s efforts to accommodate them the b=
orrower will be unable to pay, and Goldman Sachs is going to, gently and po=
litely and regretfully, take them to court for the money, and there are goi=
ng to be one million headlines like =E2=80=9CWALL STREET TRADING TITAN GOLD=
MAN SACHS SUES CANCER-STRICKEN SINGLE MOTHER FOR HER LAST PENNY.=E2=80=9D=
=C2=A0I don=E2=80=99t know man,  I don=E2=80=99t know:Goldman Sachs Group I=
nc. agreed to buy GreenSky Inc. for about $2.24 billion, adding to its Marc=
us consumer-banking platform a company that offers payment plans to custome=
rs with home-improvement projects or health-care needs.Aren=E2=80=99t you t=
empted to go borrow a bunch of money from Goldman GreenSky to redo your kit=
chen, and then not pay them back? What are they gonna do?GreenSky: Hi we we=
re calling about your loan?You: Sure.GreenSky: Were you planning to pay it =
back?You: Oh no, no no no no no. No. No indeed, not at all.GreenSky:=C2=A0H=
mm. Well of course we respect your viewpoint and want to work with you here=
,=C2=A0but I must warn you that if you do not pay back your loan we may hav=
e to report you to credit agencies and perhaps even sue you.You: Don=E2=80=
=99t you work for Goldman Sachs?GreenSky: Technically, yes, as of Tuesday.Y=
ou: I am just a regular Main Street borrower trying to get by in this econo=
my and install nice quartz=C2=A0counters, and GREAT VAMPIRE SQUID GOLDMAN S=
ACHS IS RELENTLESSLY JAMMING ITS BLOOD FUNNEL INTO ME?GreenSky: You did bor=
row the money. Like a month ago.You:=C2=A0If I ever hear from you again I a=
m going to go to the press.The press:=C2=A0Hi, sorry, I could not help over=
hearing. Did you say that Goldman Sachs was threatening you?=C2=A0This is o=
bviously not legal or financial or interior-decorating=C2=A0or media-relati=
ons advice but, man, I do feel like Goldman=E2=80=99s consumer buy-now-pay-=
later kitchen-remodeling-and-medical-expenses business=C2=A0is gonna be rea=
l gentle in its collection efforts for a while.[4]I could be wrong, though;=
 my associations with Goldman come largely from working there (disclosure![=
5]) before, during and after the global financial crisis of 2008. For a whi=
le after the crisis Goldman=C2=A0was by far the most publicly hated bank (t=
he vampire squid thing, etc.), for reasons that were pretty mysterious to m=
e but that might have had something to do with the fact that it=C2=A0didn=
=E2=80=99t=C2=A0have much of a Main Street presence. Nobody had a checking =
account at Goldman, nobody bought their house with a Goldman mortgage, so n=
obody had a face to put to the Goldman name and everyone just assumed they =
were evil.Getting deeper into the consumer business, besides being a busine=
ss move, is an interesting public-relations choice for Goldman, a gamble th=
at people will feel fonder about Goldman if they actually interact with it.=
 I don=E2=80=99t know that that=E2=80=99s generally true of banks! But that=
=E2=80=99s an opportunity too: You don=E2=80=99t have to be the nicest bank=
; you just have to be nicer, in the public mind, than, you know,  Wells Far=
go & Co.=C2=A0If you don=E2=80=99t do any consumer lending maybe that is ha=
rd; if you do some, maybe the bar is low.

      YOLO
   =20
 =20
Speaking of things that are not investing or legal or media-relations advic=
e:Robinhood Markets Inc., the go-to trading app for young investors, wants =
its user base to get even younger.The digital brokerage is kicking off a na=
tionwide marketing campaign Wednesday that is designed to turn more college=
 students into Robinhood customers. Robinhood will give students who sign u=
p for brokerage accounts using their school email address $15 to trade, and=
 enter them into a $20,000 giveaway. Robinhood executives will tour campuse=
s of community colleges and historically black colleges and universities th=
is fall. ...Aparna Chennapragada, Robinhood=E2=80=99s product chief, said t=
hat the marketing push is a continuation of Robinhood=E2=80=99s long-term m=
ission to make investing accessible to people who hadn=E2=80=99t historical=
ly participated in the markets. The campaign, she said, is about =E2=80=9Cm=
eeting the next generation where they are=E2=80=9D and communicating that b=
y starting young, college students can enjoy the benefits of compound retur=
ns over decades.Courting students is tricky for Robinhood. Critics say the =
app has turned trading into a game-like experience that encourages unsophis=
ticated investors to take risks they don=E2=80=99t understand.Yes, if you a=
re 18 and you buy weekly-expiry out-of-the-money call options on GameStop C=
orp. every week from now until you retire you will definitely compound at a=
 rate of =E2=80=A6 no honestly I can see the appeal here? For many American=
s, college is pretty much going to be the lifetime low point of their net w=
orth: They have no money, aren=E2=80=99t earning anything, and have just ta=
ken out tens of thousands of dollars of debt. So they can=E2=80=99t get all=
 that much=C2=A0worse=C2=A0off by day-trading Dogecoin and YOLOing weekly G=
ameStop options.[6] It is an inexpensive and=C2=A0appropriately timed=C2=A0=
form of investor education: Better to do your wild day-trading=C2=A0with a =
tiny account in college, get burned, get it out of your system, and then ha=
ve 40 sadder and wiser years to save for retirement. The way to become a so=
phisticated investor taking risks that you do understand is mostly to start=
 as an unsophisticated investor, take risks you don=E2=80=99t understand, l=
ose money and learn. Might as well get started on that cheap and early.

      Coinbase Borrow
   =20
 =20
We  talked last week=C2=A0about how Coinbase Global Inc. tried to roll out =
a crypto lending program in which its customers would lend it money, it wou=
ld pay them 4% interest, and it would use the money to, presumably, fund lo=
ans to other customers. This was shut down by the SEC, which told Coinbase =
that if it did this program it would need to register it as a security. Coi=
nbase complained a lot. Anyway:Junk-bond investors gave cryptocurrencies th=
eir biggest endorsement yet as Coinbase Global Inc. sold $2 billion of debt=
.Demand was so high -- at least $7 billion of orders poured in -- that the =
crypto behemoth was able to boost the deal=E2=80=99s size from $1.5 billion=
, according to a person with knowledge of the matter.Equal amounts of seven=
- and 10-year bonds were sold at interest rates of 3.375% and 3.625%, respe=
ctively, lower than the initially discussed borrowing costs, other people f=
amiliar with the situation said.So=C2=A0that=E2=80=99s cheaper than the len=
ding program, huh? And for a longer term? And, uh, those bonds are definite=
ly securities? (Privately placed Rule 144A securities, but still.) The syst=
em works I guess.

      NFT insider trading
   =20
 =20
Oh sure sure sure:In a statement issued Wednesday morning, leading non-fung=
ible token (NFT) marketplace OpenSea said it uncovered evidence of insider =
trading by one of its employees.=E2=80=9CYesterday we learned that one of o=
ur employees purchased items that they knew were set to display on our fron=
t page before they appeared there publicly,=E2=80=9D the statement reads. =
=E2=80=A6Allegations of insider trading at OpenSea appeared last night, cou=
rtesy of a Twitter account called @ZuwuTV, and it quickly went viral.Here i=
s the statement. Here is the viral tweet, which alleges that=C2=A0an OpenSe=
a employee =E2=80=9Chas a few secret wallets that appears to buy your front=
 page drops before they are listed, then sells them shortly after the front=
-page-hype spike for profits, and then tumbles them back to his main wallet=
 with his punk on it.=E2=80=9DI just want to say how, like,=C2=A0drab this =
is. This isn=E2=80=99t =E2=80=9Cinsider trading=E2=80=9D in that he knows s=
omething about these tokens that no one else knows. There is nothing to kno=
w about these tokens! It=E2=80=99s insider trading in that he knows they=E2=
=80=99re going to be=C2=A0heavily advertised=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0on the fro=
nt page of OpenSea =E2=80=94=C2=A0before anyone else does. If your model of=
 NFTs is, like, =E2=80=9Cthese things are good and people want them because=
 they are a new form of art collecting and value accumulation, they have gr=
eat aesthetic appeal, and they point a way to a technological future where =
physical goods are owned and traded on the blockchain,=E2=80=9D who cares. =
If your model of NFTs is, like, =E2=80=9Cevery day some new NFT gets hyped =
and everyone buys it and it goes up, and the next day a different NFT gets =
hyped and goes up, and it is all a game of hype and market manipulation=E2=
=80=9D then, sure, this guy won the game.I  wrote yesterday that =E2=80=9Ca=
 lot of actually existing NFTs have almost no consumption value outside of =
participating in a bubble.=E2=80=9D Right? If I were an insider-trading=C2=
=A0OpenSea employee, I=E2=80=99d mint NFTs representing each of my insider =
trades on NFTs, and sell those.

mpliance with the federal securities laws. For example, they represented th=

ence estimates. They understood that, without these representations, tradin=

elligence estimates in making their trading decisions. During most of the r=
elevant period, however, App Annie did not have effective internal controls=
 and in fact used certain public companies=E2=80=99 Connect Data to generat=

l often publish some sort of dissenting statement on the SEC website, but i=
n most cases a tweet is probably sufficient so here we are.[4] Meanwhile, u=
h: =E2=80=9CGreenSky in July reached a settlement with the Consumer Financi=
al Protection Bureau to resolve what the regulator called =E2=80=98careless=
 business and customer service practices=E2=80=99 that allowed merchants an=
d contractors to take out loans on behalf of thousands of customers who did=
n=E2=80=99t ask for them. GreenSky agreed to cancel up to $9 million in out=
standing loans and pay a $2.5 million penalty.=E2=80=9D Replace the word =
=E2=80=9CGreenSky=E2=80=9D there with the words =E2=80=9CGoldman Sachs=E2=

] Also I have a Marcus savings account? I don=E2=80=99t know that using a s=
tandard banking product is the sort of thing that one discloses but perhaps=
 it is relevant. I have not taken out a kitchen remodeling loan from Goldma=
n, but if I ever do need a kitchen remodeling loan, I will probably go to G=
oldman GreenSky, send them a copy of this column, and let you all know how =
that goes.=C2=A0[6] There is a sad exception to this. From the same Wall St=
reet Journal article: =E2=80=9CRobinhood settled a wrongful-death lawsuit e=
arlier this year brought by the family of a college student who took his ow=
n life after believing he racked up big trading losses on Robinhood.=E2=80=
=9D Obviously Robinhood should (1) make it impossible to rack up big tradin=
g losses with a small account and (2) send people accurate account statemen=
ts; this seems like a low bar but Robinhood has sometimes  failed to clear =

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong><em> Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow, back on Friday.</em></p><table class=3D"header" =
width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">App Annie</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is a thing that, I suspect, keeps=
 a lot of hedge fund lawyers up at night. Your hedge fund is in the busines=
s of getting =E2=80=9Cedge.=E2=80=9D It wants to know things that other peo=
ple do not know, in order to buy the stocks that will go up. One way to get=
 edge is to bribe the assistant treasurer of a public company to give you t=
he company=E2=80=99s earnings release=C2=A0in advance, but this is strongly=
 disfavored and your hedge fund has effective=C2=A0policies against it. Ano=
ther way to get edge, quite popular these days, is to buy =E2=80=9Calternat=
ive data.=E2=80=9D Somebody has a satellite and they fly it over mall parki=
ng lots and count up the cars in front of each store, and you pay them and =
they give you=C2=A0those numbers, and then you buy the stocks of the stores=
 with lots of cars in front of them, and then a month later those stores an=
nounce good earnings and you make a profit.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"=
>The car-counting is a classic story of alternative data, but the way alter=
native data mostly works in practice is that a lot of people use apps on th=
eir mobile phones, and everyone involved in the mobile app business is harv=
esting data and frantically selling it to each other and to hedge funds. So=
 basically the =E2=80=9Calternative data=E2=80=9D that your hedge fund buys=
 is of the form =E2=80=9Cthis many people used this company=E2=80=99s app,=
=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cpeople spent this much on this company=E2=80=99s prod=
ucts through apps,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0or =E2=80=9Cphone location data shows that=
 this many people walked into this store,=E2=80=9D or whatever. Someone in =
the app business collects that data and then sells it to hedge funds, inclu=
ding yours. And you use this mobile-app data to get a sense of how many peo=
ple are interacting with some company, so you can buy or sell that company=
=E2=80=99s stock.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now, if you bribe the com=
pany=E2=80=99s assistant treasurer to get an early peek at an=C2=A0earnings=
 release, that is clearly =E2=80=9Cinsider trading.=E2=80=9D It is illegal,=
 the bad kind of edge. As I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/25050529.260552/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2Fyd=
GljbGVzLzIwMTktMDMtMTMveW91LWhhdmUtdG8tcGF5LXRoZS1yaWdodC1wZXJzb24/607f07ce=
ef4b8524a319a23cB54afbb2c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;"> like to say</a>, insider trading is not about <em>fairnes=
s</em>; it=E2=80=99s about <em>theft</em>.=C2=A0Someone (the company) owns =
the information (the earnings release); somebody else (the assistant treasu=
rer) takes it, in breach of a duty to the owners of the information,=C2=A0a=
nd gives it to you illegitimately. So you are trading on illegally obtained=
 information and get in trouble.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What about=
 that mobile-app data that you=E2=80=99re buying from an alternative data p=
rovider? Well, it is=C2=A0<em>nonpublic=C2=A0</em>information: Not everyone=
 has it, which is why you want it; it gives you an edge.[1] But the questio=
n is whether you got it illegitimately, which means, whether the data broke=
r owned the data and had the right to sell it to you. I think that is a com=
plicated question? Roughly speaking:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decim=
al; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px=
;">If the people using the apps give the apps permission to track them, and=
 the companies that make the apps give permission to the aggregators to agg=
regate them, and the aggregators give permission to the data vendors=C2=A0t=
o sell their data, and the vendors sell the data to you, fine, great. Every=
body has consented to the use of their information, so you can use it freel=
y.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">If at any point in that chain =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0which can have more or fewer links than I wrote=C2=A0in the =
previous paragraph =E2=80=94=C2=A0someone=C2=A0<em>doesn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</e=
m>give permission, then the data is misappropriated. If you use that data i=
n your trading, you are insider trading,=C2=A0trading based on misappropria=
ted material nonpublic information. Also the person doing the misappropriat=
ing =E2=80=94=C2=A0the person passing along data without permission =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0is also engaged in insider trading, if they know that you=E2=80=99=
re going to be using it to trade.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So =
if an app says =E2=80=9Cwe don=E2=80=99t sell your data,=E2=80=9D and then =
it sells that data to hedge funds, that data is arguably misappropriated? A=
rguably it is material nonpublic information that belongs=C2=A0<em>to the u=
sers of the app</em>, and is sold to hedge funds without their permission? =
Or if people click a box in an app saying =E2=80=9Csure go nuts sell my dat=
a to anyone,=E2=80=9D and the app sells the data to some third-party servic=
e, but the app=E2=80=99s contract with the third-party service says =E2=80=
=9Cdon=E2=80=99t sell any of this data, in an identifiable form, to hedge f=
unds,=E2=80=9D and the service does that, then, again, that=E2=80=99s misap=
propriation and possible insider trading.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A=
nd so as the general counsel of a hedge fund you do due diligence, and you =
ask the alternative data provider questions like =E2=80=9Cdid everyone give=
 informed consent to you collecting this data and selling it to me, a hedge=
 fund?=E2=80=9D And they say yes, and you hope they=E2=80=99re telling the =
truth, and you examine their contracts and the apps=E2=80=99 terms of servi=
ce and so forth to make sure that the chain of data is reasonably clean. Bu=
t you probably always worry a bit because, I don=E2=80=99t know, the whole =
business of apps selling data to each other does seem like it is not founde=
d on best practices of fully informed consent?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">Yesterday the=C2=A0U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission brought a pr=
etty important <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/250=
50529.260552/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMS0xNz=
Y/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5c5cfc87" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"it=
emscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;">enforcement action</a> against a company called =
App Annie Inc. and its former chief executive officer, Bertrand Schmitt; Sc=
hmitt and App Annie settled for about $10 million. App Annie got data from =
apps and sold it to hedge funds without using best practices of fully infor=
med consent:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">According to the S=
EC's order, App Annie is one of the largest sellers of market data on mobil=
e app performance, including estimates on the number of times a particular =
company's app is downloaded, how often it's used, and the amount of revenue=
 the app generates for the company. Trading firms commonly refer to this ty=
pe of information as "alternative data" because it is not contained within =
companies' financial statements or other traditional data sources. The orde=
r finds that App Annie and Schmitt understood that companies would only sha=
re their confidential app performance data with App Annie if it promised no=
t to disclose their data to third parties, and as a result App Annie and Sc=
hmitt assured companies that their data would be aggregated and anonymized =
before being used by a statistical model to generate estimates of app perfo=
rmance. Contrary to these representations, the order finds that from late 2=
014 through mid-2018, App Annie used non-aggregated and non-anonymized data=
 to alter its model-generated estimates to make them more valuable to sell =
to trading firms.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The order further finds t=
hat App Annie and Schmitt misrepresented to their trading firm customers th=
at App Annie generated the estimates in a way that was consistent with the =
consents it obtained from companies that shared their confidential data, an=
d that App Annie had effective internal controls to prevent the misuse of c=
onfidential data and to ensure that it was in compliance with the federal s=
ecurities laws. According to the SEC's order, App Annie and Schmitt were aw=
are that trading firm customers were making investment decisions based on A=
pp Annie's estimates, and App Annie also shared ideas for how the trading f=
irms could use the estimates to trade ahead of upcoming earnings announceme=
nts.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is a little hard to te=
ll what people=C2=A0<em>thought=C2=A0</em>was going on. Basically if you ar=
e a company with an app, you can use an App Annie product for free to, like=
, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25050529.260552/=
aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2FkbWluLzIwMjEvMzQtOTI5NzUucGRm/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB41e0648a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;">see how good your app is</a>:</p><blockquote><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">App Annie provides a free analytics product called =
=E2=80=9CConnect=E2=80=9D to companies that offer apps, including public co=
mpanies, which enables those companies to track how their apps are performi=
ng. As a condition of their use of Connect, those companies provide App Ann=
ie with their app store credentials to allow App Annie to collect their con=
fidential app performance metrics (=E2=80=9CConnect Data=E2=80=9D). App Ann=
ie tells Connect users that it will generate estimates of app performance u=
sing their Connect Data, but that it will only use Connect Data in aggregat=
ed and anonymized form to generate those estimates.</p></blockquote><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And if you are a hedge fund you can buy App Annie=E2=
=80=99s =E2=80=9Cestimates=E2=80=9D of app performance, which you can then =
use as part of your own modeling of the company=E2=80=99s financial perform=
ance:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">App Annie=E2=80=99s busin=
ess model relied on selling estimates of how apps belonging to certain comp=
anies were performing. App Annie sold these estimates through a paid subscr=
iption product called =E2=80=9CIntelligence,=E2=80=9D which included =E2=80=
=9CStore Intelligence=E2=80=9D (for estimates of app revenue and app downlo=
ads) and =E2=80=9CUsage Intelligence=E2=80=9D (for estimates of app usage).=
 Paying subscribers were companies that offered apps and trading firms, suc=
h as hedge funds. During the relevant period, substantially all of App Anni=
e=E2=80=99s revenue was derived from selling Intelligence estimates to thes=
e subscribers. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">App Annie=E2=80=99=
s marketing materials encouraged =E2=80=9Cinvestors=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cf=
inance professionals=E2=80=9D to use Intelligence estimates to =E2=80=9Cinf=
orm their investment strategy.=E2=80=9D App Annie=E2=80=99s website claimed=
 that investors could =E2=80=9Cmake more informed decisions about existing =
positions=E2=80=9D by =E2=80=9Cbenchmark[ing] the performance of public app=
 companies against key metrics including user engagement, revenue and growt=
h.=E2=80=9D Schmitt occasionally participated in meetings with existing and=
 prospective trading firm subscribers, and some of the materials used in th=
ese meetings pitched that Intelligence estimates could help with =E2=80=9Cf=
inancial modeling,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Csharpen earnings forecasts with estima=
ted downloads and revenue inputs,=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cidentify investment=
 opportunities.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As fa=
r as I can tell these =E2=80=9Cestimates=E2=80=9D worked like this:</p><ol =
style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09=
<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">App Annie used aggregated anonymous data =
from the apps it tracks to build a statistical model of how apps with certa=
in characteristics perform.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It use=
d that model to generate an estimate of how each particular company=E2=80=
=99s app performed.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Then it checke=
d that estimate against=C2=A0<em>how the app actually performed, which it k=
new, from collecting=C2=A0that app=E2=80=99s data</em>, and corrected the e=
stimate to reflect the actual performance.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
x 0;">Arguably steps (1) and (2) were unnecessary, from a performance-estim=
ation standpoint? Like, you just have the answer; why build a statistical m=
odel to estimate it?=C2=A0But they were important from a selling-data-to-he=
dge-funds standpoint: App Annie =E2=80=9Crepresented to Intelligence subscr=
ibers that Intelligence estimates were generated through a statistical mode=
l that used aggregated and anonymized Connect Data, and that Connect users =
had consented to App Annie using their confidential data in this way.=E2=80=
=9D And in fact the Connect users =E2=80=94=C2=A0the companies =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0<em>had=C2=A0</em>consented to App Annie using their data in that way.[2=
] But that=E2=80=99s not how App Annie used it, or at least, not the only w=
ay:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Beginning at least in 2014,=
 in an effort to make the Intelligence estimates closer to the actual app p=
erformance metrics, App Annie created a manual process whereby a =E2=80=9CD=
elivery Team,=E2=80=9D which consisted of a subset of App Annie engineers b=
ased in Beijing, China, made manual alterations to estimates generated by t=
he statistical model before they were delivered to Intelligence subscribers=
. Schmitt was aware of and approved the creation and use of this manual alt=
eration process.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Delivery Team manually=
 altered the estimates for apps that were of greatest interest to App Annie=
=E2=80=99s highest-paying subscribers. When App Annie received complaints f=
rom subscribers about the inaccuracy of a particular company=E2=80=99s app =
performance estimates, the Delivery Team was tasked with improving the esti=
mates through this manual process.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">To=
 make these manual alterations, the Delivery Team looked at confidential Co=
nnect Data, including public company app performance data. The Delivery Tea=
m was not trained or supervised by anyone in the Company=E2=80=99s Data Sci=
ence group and did not document which estimates were adjusted or what chang=
es were made. There was no statistical basis for these post-model alteratio=
ns. The only purpose of these alterations was to make the estimates closer =
to the actual metrics.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Schmitt approved the=
 creation and use of this manual alteration process because he believed it =
was cheaper and more effective at making the estimates closer to the actual=
 results than a process that would have complied with the Connect Terms of =
Service, such as having data scientists research and implement improvements=
 to the statistical model itself.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">I =E2=80=A6=C2=A0honestly I sort of sympathize? If you know how m=
uch revenue a company got through its app, it seems a bit baroque to have a=
 team of data scientists build a statistical model based on data from hundr=
eds of apps, and refine that model in order to make it more accurately esti=
mate how much revenue the company got through its app, when you can just lo=
ok at the answer. Like, yes, telling people the actual data=C2=A0<em>is=C2=
=A0</em>cheaper and more effective at making the estimates closer to the ac=
tual results than some more complicated process that does not involve=C2=A0=
<em>looking at the actual results.=C2=A0</em>This is a very simple business=
, really, that was rendered a lot more complicated by the desire to wave va=
guely in the direction of securities-law compliance.</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">Anyway it=E2=80=99s all fine now:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">In June 2018, after the Company had learned of the SEC=E2=80=
=99s investigation, App Annie discontinued all post-model estimate alterati=
on practices based on non-aggregated and non-anonymized Connect Data. The C=
ompany also began excluding all public company data from its statistical mo=
del, consistent with its representations to Intelligence subscribers and Co=
nnect users. Around the same time, Schmitt resigned and the Board replaced =
him as CEO.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">App Annie eventually implemente=
d a new version of the statistical model underlying the Store Intelligence =
product that had been proposed by the Chief Data Scientist in or around mid=
-2016.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I actually feel like if=
 you are a hedge fund lawyer this case probably comes as a relief to you? L=
ike, yes, you were buying illegal alternative data, but (1) the data was go=
od and (2)=C2=A0<em>you=C2=A0</em>didn=E2=80=99t get in trouble. App Annie =
got in trouble, for lying to you, rather than you getting in trouble, for b=
elieving them.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Which seems fair and good, d=
on=E2=80=99t get me wrong, but <em>you were the one doing the trading</em>.=
 It is not necessarily=C2=A0<em>intuitive=C2=A0</em>that an app-performance=
-analytics-and-data-aggregation company would get in trouble for=C2=A0<em>s=
ecurities fraud=C2=A0</em>for using non-aggregated data in its estimates of=
 app performance. App Annie wasn=E2=80=99t buying or selling securities. SE=
C Commissioner <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/250=
50529.260552/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9IZXN0ZXJQZWlyY2Uvc3RhdHVzLzE0Mzc4Nj=
IzNzg3MTMwOTYxOTc/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe04137f5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink"=
 itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !im=
portant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bot=
tom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Hester Peirce tweeted in dissent=
</a>: =E2=80=9CThis settlement stretches the =E2=80=98in connection with th=
e purchase and sale of securities=E2=80=99 requirement under 10b/10b-5 beyo=
nd where I think it should go.=E2=80=9D[3] I disagree: I think that this is=
 a=C2=A0subtle insider trading case, and the SEC was clever=C2=A0to pursue =
it and to correctly=C2=A0identify=C2=A0the bad guy (App Annie, which lied t=
o hedge funds to sell illegal data, not the hedge funds, which were deceive=
d and bought the data). But it is not obvious. And I suppose that if you ar=
e a hedge fund lawyer, now you are on notice that you should be asking your=
 alternative data providers some tougher questions.</p><table class=3D"live=
intent-sponsored-content" width=3D"auto" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0=
">
<tr>

16px 0;">Courting students is tricky for Robinhood. Critics say the app has=
